As David flees Jerusalem after his son Absalom's rebellion, he encounters two scenes that expose the fragility of human loyalty and the sovereignty of God over his suffering. Ziba appears with generous provision and a calculated lie—claiming that Mephibosheth, the crippled heir of Saul, intends to reclaim his grandfather's throne. David, in his flight and vulnerability, accepts the lie without verification and grants Ziba all of Mephibosheth's property. The moment reveals how easily fear can cloud judgment, even in a man described as wise. But it also shows something deeper: David is experiencing what he has inflicted on others. The man who shed blood to consolidate his kingdom now loses his kingdom through his own son. The consequences of his sin are not erased; they are being lived out in real time.

The true test of David's character comes when Shimei, a relative of Saul's house, curses him openly and violently. Throwing stones and dust, Shimei accuses David of bloodshed and declares that God Himself has turned the kingdom over to Absalom. Everything in this moment invites David to crush his enemy—his guards are loyal, Abishai begs for permission to execute Shimei, and the king has every power to silence him. Yet David refuses. His response cuts to the heart of what submission to God actually means: "If He curses me because the LORD told him, 'Curse David,' who can ask, 'Why did you do this?'" David does not defend himself with the claim of innocence. Instead, he accepts that God may be speaking through Shimei's very accusations. He sees his son's rebellion and this stranger's curses as instruments in God's hand, and rather than fight them, he yields to them. He even hopes that God will see his affliction and "repay me with good for the cursing I receive today."

While David walks in this costly surrender, Absalom settles into Jerusalem and receives the counsel of Ahithophel, a man whose wisdom is "like the consultation of the word of God." Ahithophel advises Absalom to sleep with his father's concubines publicly—an act that would shame David irreparably and bind Absalom's followers to him through the point of no return. The plan succeeds perfectly. Yet here Scripture whispers a warning: not all wisdom that seems like the word of God actually comes from God. Ahithophel's counsel is humanly brilliant and strategically devastating, but it operates entirely within the realm of human cunning. David's faith, by contrast, rests on something no strategy can touch: the belief that God sees his suffering and will vindicate him. As you read this chapter, ask yourself which kind of wisdom you are pursuing—the wisdom that wins wars or the wisdom that trusts God in the ruins.
